## [03:02 - 06:02]

pushing it down the middle of the court and you're literally just hitting it in this area, you know, kind of like the pusher, that's not going to build a ton of confidence because you've gotten away from your normal game plan and you're literally just kind of, you know, maybe dinking the ball a little bit, just pushing so you don't miss. Not the biggest confidence booster. So the tactic you would use to play your game and play a higher percentage tennis is shrinking the court. And this tactic was taught to me by Frank Salazar, who is one of my mentors, good friend of mine and the 2008 United States Olympic Committee coach of the year, one of the best coaches in the U.S. Tactic and this tactic is real simple. You take the sidelines and the baseline and you just move them in a little bit. You shrink the court, you know, maybe by a couple of feet, it's the exact distance will depend on how, how errant you might be. But by doing that, you're not going to miss wide or deep and you can still play your game. And that's key. You're still playing your game. Maybe you're still using the Agassi method, for example. We talked about that in a previous video where you're moving the ball side to side. And it's just, you know, it's a higher percentage game. So confidence is getting the ball in, but you're also playing your game and that's really what's going to boost your confidence back up. And once that happens, you can expand the court back to its normal boundaries. But that, this tactic prevents you from simply pushing in the interest of getting the ball in. And it's tough to, to be real confident when you just totally get away from your game plan and simply try and get the ball in the court. And this is also a good tactic to use in a pressure situation. It was the same thing he did, same modification, same adjustment he made, you know, back in the, that he used back in the Aussie Open, where he started to use the slice a lot.
